title: 40_sequential_waterfall_fetches.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Dashboard Loads Slowly — Three Sequential Fetches

A dashboard loads three independent data sources: user profile (300ms), stats (400ms), and notification feed (250ms). The three fetches are independent — none depends on the result of another. Yet they run one after the other, adding up to ~950ms.

A user with a slow connection reports the dashboard takes over a second to show anything. A performance audit flags this as an **async waterfall** — sequential awaits where parallel execution is possible.

Fix the fetches to run in parallel so the dashboard loads in the time of the slowest single request (~400ms).
traceback:
Network tab (waterfall view):
  t=0ms:    GET /api/users/1         starts
  t=300ms:  GET /api/users/1         resolves ← then stats starts
  t=300ms:  GET /api/users/1/stats   starts
  t=700ms:  GET /api/users/1/stats   resolves ← then feed starts
  t=700ms:  GET /api/users/1/feed    starts
  t=950ms:  GET /api/users/1/feed    resolves

Total: 950ms. User waits nearly 1 second before anything renders.
correctOutput:
Network tab (parallel):
  t=0ms:    GET /api/users/1       starts
  t=0ms:    GET /api/users/1/stats starts
  t=0ms:    GET /api/users/1/feed  starts
  t=250ms:  feed resolves
  t=300ms:  profile resolves
  t=400ms:  stats resolves → Promise.all resolves

Total: 400ms — max of (300, 400, 250). 58% faster.

options.label: Start all three fetches with `Promise.all` so they run concurrently
options.resultLog:
Network tab:
  t=0ms:   all three requests start simultaneously
  t=400ms: Promise.all resolves (slowest was stats at 400ms)

Total: 400ms — 58% faster than sequential.
All three data values are available for destructuring.
options.rationale: `Promise.all` starts all promises immediately and waits for all to resolve. Since none of these fetches depend on each other, they can run at the same time. The total wait time is the slowest individual request (~400ms), not the sum (~950ms). For independent fetches, `Promise.all` is always faster than sequential `await`.

options.label: Start all three fetches manually, then await each result separately
options.resultLog:
Network tab:
  t=0ms:   all three requests start simultaneously
  t=400ms: all resolved

Performance: same as Promise.all — all start at t=0.

But: the code is more verbose. Promise.all with destructuring
is the idiomatic approach. Both are correct; Promise.all is preferred.
options.rationale: Starting all three promises before awaiting them is correct — they run in parallel because `fetch()` starts the request immediately (before `await`). The performance is identical to `Promise.all`. However, `Promise.all` with array destructuring is the idiomatic, concise form and reads as 'these three things happen together'. The manual approach works but is more verbose.

options.label: Fetch profile first, then use its `id` to fetch stats and feed in parallel
options.resultLog:
Network tab:
  t=0ms:    profile starts
  t=300ms:  profile resolves → stats and feed start in parallel
  t=300ms:  stats and feed start
  t=700ms:  Promise.all resolves (stats is slowest at 400ms)

Total: 700ms (300 sequential + 400 parallel).
Faster than fully sequential (950ms) but slower than fully parallel (400ms).

This pattern IS correct when stats/feed DEPEND on profile data.
In this mission, all three are independent — use full parallel.
options.rationale: Fetching profile first, then using its result to fetch the remaining data, is correct when there is a real dependency (stats endpoint needs the profile ID). But if the `userId` parameter is enough for all three endpoints (as in the original code), fetching profile first adds an unnecessary 300ms sequential delay. Always identify real dependencies before sequencing requests.

hints: These three fetches are independent — none uses the result of another. Sequential `await` forces them into a queue. `fetch()` starts the request immediately; `await` only waits for the result. You can start all three before awaiting any of them.

`Promise.all([p1, p2, p3])` starts all three promises at once and resolves when all complete. The total time is the duration of the slowest request, not the sum. Replace the three sequential awaits with one `await Promise.all([...])` and destructure the results.
recruiterReview: Correct. Async waterfalls are a real performance problem in production dashboards — they are easy to write accidentally and hard to spot in code review because each `await` line looks benign in isolation. The manual-promises distractor is technically correct but less idiomatic. The profile-first distractor is the right pattern for when there IS a dependency — identifying when sequencing IS justified versus when it IS an accident is the key judgment. ✅
